title: Valley-polarized Orbital and Spin Magnetism Induced by Femtosecond Optical Pulses in Two-Dimensional Semiconductors
authors: M. S. Mrudul, Peter M. Oppeneer
abstract: We theoretically investigate the ultrafast generation of spin and orbital magnetism in a two-dimensional gapped Dirac system with spin-orbit coupling. This system is representative of two-dimensional hexagonal semiconductors, such as transition-metal dichalcogenides that exhibit valley-selective optical selection rules arising from the valley-contrasting magnetic texture of their band structure. Using a time-dependent density-matrix formalism, we demonstrate that circularly polarized laser pulses generate nonequilibrium magnetization under both resonant and multiphoton resonant conditions. We show that the induced spin and orbital magnetic moments can be distinctly controlled via the photon energy and polarization of the driving field. Furthermore, spin and orbital dynamics originate from fundamentally different light-matter coupling mechanisms, leading to qualitatively dissimilar temporal behaviors. The orbital magnetic moment couples directly to the external electric field, resulting in faster dynamics and pronounced Rabi-like oscillations, whereas the spin response develops gradually through spin-orbit coupling. Consequently, orbital dynamics is significantly more sensitive to electron-hole dephasing than the spin response. Our results highlight the importance of properly accounting for orbital contributions in future technologies that utilize femtosecond control of magnetism.
summary: This theoretical work models how femtosecond laser pulses generate non-equilibrium spin and orbital magnetism in 2D semiconductors like TMDs. It shows that orbital and spin responses are governed by distinct physical mechanisms, leading to different temporal behaviors. The findings highlight the crucial role of orbital contributions in future ultrafast spintronic and magnetoelectronic devices.
main_problem: The study investigates the ultrafast generation and distinct dynamics of spin and orbital magnetism in two-dimensional (2D) semiconductors using femtosecond optical pulses.
main_result: The paper demonstrates that orbital and spin magnetic moments evolve via fundamentally different light-matter coupling mechanisms, allowing for selective control and distinct temporal dynamics.
method: The dynamics are modeled using a time-dependent density-matrix formalism solved numerically, incorporating electron-hole dephasing effects.
model_or_system: The system is a 2D gapped Dirac semiconductor, representative of TMDs, modeled by a tight-binding Hamiltonian incorporating spin-orbit coupling. The interaction is driven by circularly polarized femtosecond laser pulses.
key_observables: Time evolution of spin ($\mathbf{m}_{	ext{spin}}$) and orbital ($\mathbf{m}_{	ext{orb}}$) magnetization, and the dependence of remnant magnetization on photon energy.
important_parameters: Laser pulse duration (100 fs), peak intensity ($10^{11} 	ext{ W/cm}^2$), and specific photon energies relative to the band gap ($\Delta_g$) and spin-flip gap ($\Delta_{sf}g$).
main_assumptions: The analysis relies on the minimal coupling in the dipole approximation and includes decoherence effects ($T_2$).
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the projected components of orbital and spin magnetic moments, the time evolution of magnetization under different excitation conditions, and the connection between remnant magnetism and valley-polarized conduction band populations.
paper_structure: The paper systematically analyzes the generation of magnetization using time-dependent density matrix equations, comparing resonant vs. multiphoton excitation regimes, and contrasting the distinct dynamics of orbital (Rabi-like, fast) versus spin (gradual, SOC-mediated) responses.
why_interesting: The explicit separation and comparison of orbital and spin dynamics, particularly the role of direct electric field coupling versus SOC mediation, provides a rich platform for understanding ultrafast quantum control in condensed matter systems.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Electronic band structure of an archetypal 2D semi- conductor. (a) Orbital and (b) spin components of magnetic moments (mnk,z) projected onto the band structure along the high-symmetry directions. The inset in (a) shows the high- symmetry points in the first Brillouin zone.

FIG. 3. (a) Laser-driven femtosecond dynamics of orbital (orange) and spin (blue) magnetization under laser pulses with ¯hω ≈∆sf g and different electric field strengths, indi- cated by the transparency of the line plots. (b) Laser-driven dynamics of magnetization with an electron-hole dephasing time T2 of 10 fs (blue for spin and green for orbital contri- butions) and in the absence of dephasing (∞; yellow dashed for spin and red dashed for orbital). The peak laser intensity is I0 = 1011 W/cm2, corresponding to the field amplitude E0 = 8.7 MV/cm.

FIG. 2. Laser-driven dynamics of the orbital (left panels) and spin (right panels) magnetization. Red and blue curves correspond to magnetization induced by left (σ−) and right (σ+) circularly polarized laser pulses, respectively. The top panels show the response to a laser pulse with photon energy 3.1 eV (¯hω ≈∆g), while the bottom panels correspond to excitation at 4.1 eV (¯hω ≈∆sf g ). The grey shaded regions indicate the temporal profile of the 100 fs long laser pulse with a peak intensity of 1011 W/cm2.

FIG. 4. (a) Laser-driven dynamics of the orbital (orange) and spin (light blue) magnetization for a laser pulse with photon energy ¯hω = 2.1 eV. (b) Electron population in the first conduction band (CB1) in reciprocal space at the end of the laser pulse. (c) Contours in the first Brillouin zone satisfying ECB1,k −EVB1,k = 2¯hω (red) and ECB1,k −EVB2,k = 2¯hω (blue). (d) Schematic illustration of two-photon excitation induced by a right-circularly polarized laser field. The population generated in the first conduction band arises from transitions from the first valence band (VB1 →CB1, blue arrows) and the second valence band (VB2 →CB1, red arrows).

FIG. 5. Laser-induced spin (blue) and orbital (orange) rem- nant magnetization (calculated at the end of the laser pulse) as a function of the driving photon energy for (a) left- and (b) right-circularly polarized laser pulses. Panels (c) and (d) show the laser-excited electron population in the conduction bands at the end of the laser pulse, integrated around the K (red) and K′ (green) valleys, for (c) left- and (d) right-circularly polarized laser pulses. The separation of the reciprocal space unit-cell around the K and K′ valleys is illustrated in the inset of panel (c). The gray-shaded region indicates photon ener- gies above the band gap, ∆g.

Spintronics.note: The new paper directly models the ultrafast generation and dynamics of spin and orbital magnetism in 2D semiconductors using optical pulses, which is a core topic in quantum-optics driven spintronics.
